MKT.name: Foundations of Marketing Management
MKT.description: Overview of marketing concepts, tools, and analytical methods. Covers the seven key elements of the marketing mix management (product, price, promotion, distribution, people, process, and facilities), customer value, competitive analysis, marketing research, segmentation, branding, and consumer behavior.

MKT.name: Global Dimensions of Marketing
MKT.description: Systems-oriented approach examining major environmental forces affecting marketing managers. Explores marketing research, buying behavior, segmentation, targeting, and the marketing mix in a broader, international context.

MKT.name: Consumer Behavior
MKT.description: Applies concepts from economics, psychology, sociology, and anthropology to examine internal and external factors that influence the acquisition, consumption, and disposition of goods, services, and ideas.

MKT.name: Marketing Research
MKT.description: Focuses on developing information for marketing decision-making, including identifying problems, designing research, acquiring information, evaluating data, and presenting findings.

MKT.name: Sports Marketing
MKT.description: Introduction to sports marketing combining theory with practical insights. Covers strategies for promoting events, teams, and products, with a focus on fan engagement, digital channels, and sponsorships.

MKT.name: Retail Management
MKT.description: Addresses retailing as a distinct business format. Examines retail evolution, 21st-century challenges, and internet's role in retail strategy, emphasizing consumer attraction and long-term relationship development.

MKT.name: Personal Selling
MKT.description: Hands-on sales skills course covering adaptive selling, ethics, relationship and trust building, closing the sale, negotiating for win-win solutions. Includes sales competitions, case studies, and recorded presentations with professional feedback.

MKT.name: International Marketing
MKT.description: Studies essentials of international marketing, examining cultural, political, economic, and legal system differences. Investigates market entry modes, trade trends, positioning, research, and each element of the marketing mix in the international context.

MKT.name: Advertising and Integrated Marketing Communication
MKT.description: Overviews promotional activities and their effective integration. Emphasizes determining communication goals, marketing and promotional objectives, developing creative themes, testing messages, evaluating promotion effectiveness, and strategic campaign planning.

MKT.name: Services Marketing
MKT.description: Focuses on distinctive marketing challenges associated with service offerings, addressing key differences between the marketing and management of services and the marketing of goods.

MKT.name: Digital Marketing
MKT.description: Examines digital marketing for achieving business goals. Covers online consumer behavior, digital channels, campaign creation, and effectiveness tracking for B2B and B2C brands.

MKT.name: New Product Development
MKT.description: Introduces organizational stages for bringing new products to market, covering decisions from concept generation and problem-based ideation to marketing testing and launch management.

MKT.name: Marketing Internship
MKT.description: Individually supervised employment in marketing areas (retailing, advertising, sales, research). Requires minimum ten hours weekly work, faculty meetings, literature research, and substantive report preparation.

MKT.name: Business to Business Marketing
MKT.description: Investigates domestic and international activities marketing to industrial buyers, governments, and intermediaries. Supply Chain Management is a central core with emphasis on distribution, channel participants, value analysis, contracting, ethics, and pricing.

MKT.name: Marketing Policy and Problems
MKT.description: Capstone course integrating knowledge from prior marketing courses. Students conduct a situation analysis, identify opportunities and problems, formulate marketing strategies, plan and execute tactics using simulated virtual business environments.

MKT.name: Sustainability Marketing
MKT.description: Explores sustainability marketing from international perspective, assessing sustainable practices of international companies with a focus on the supply chain. Includes travel component for global experiential learning.

MKT.name: Visual Analytics for Marketing
MKT.description: Introduces marketing analytics emphasizing visual techniques for exploring and communicating data. Focuses on dynamic data interaction, visual storytelling, and analytical thinking to enhance decision-making using Excel and Tableau.

MKT.name: Marketing Analytics
MKT.description: Teaches quantitative and analytical tools for marketing decisions, including forecasting, behavioral modeling, and linear and nonlinear programming with spreadsheet and statistical software applications.

MKT.name: Sales Management
MKT.description: Hands-on sales management training including team management, manager shadowing, and professional competitions. Topics include managing conflict, goal setting, providing feedback, coaching, sales forecasting, motivating your sales team, training, compensation, recruiting, selection, performance evaluation.

MKT.name: Digital Marketing II
MKT.description: Examines developing, implementing, and analyzing digital strategy across channels. Students gain comprehensive understanding of how to develop an integrated digital marketing strategy and optimize it for multi-channel traffic acquisition. Covers SEO, SEM, display, mobile, social media, content marketing.

MKT.name: Directed Study in Marketing
MKT.description: In-depth exploration of specialized marketing areas through individualized instruction. Extensive research including primary data collection may be required, concluding with research report and presentation.

MGT.name: Management Principles and Practice
MGT.description: Introduces foundational management concepts through three areas: leadership and organizational culture, performance management and employee engagement, and high-performing teams. Uses articles, experiential exercises, case studies, simulations, and videos.

MGT.name: Operations Management
MGT.description: Covers theories and tools for managing competitive advantage in global markets. Topics include operations strategy, process design, quality management, inventory theory, and project management, balancing managerial issues with analytical techniques.

MGT.name: Global Dimensions of Operations Management
MGT.description: Two-part course addressing manufacturing and service operations globally. Section I covers foundational concepts; Section II focuses on supply chain integration, inventory management, warehouse logistics, and lean systems for competitive advantage.

MGT.name: Honors Management for Organizational Leadership
MGT.description: Develops decision-making effectiveness for future organizational leaders. Uses interactive, cooperative learning including team projects, simulations, and case analyses. Examines leading management theorists and develops interpersonal skills.

MGT.name: Organizational Behavior
MGT.description: Explores human dimensions of management emphasizing theory application to real-world problems and interpersonal skill development. Topics include motivation, leadership, group dynamics, and interpersonal communication.

MGT.name: Human Resources Management
MGT.description: In-depth study of human resources management principles, emphasizing functions managers and HR personnel must understand for developing an effective workforce.

MGT.name: International Business Management
MGT.description: Covers cultural, economic, legal, and political forces shaping international business environments. Develops knowledge and skills for cross-border management.

MGT.name: Diversity in a Global Environment
MGT.description: Frames diversity as a leverageable resource rather than a problem. Uses lectures, discussions, films, simulations, case studies, and interactive media addressing demographic workforce changes.

MGT.name: Global Dimensions of Human Resource Management
MGT.description: Addresses HR challenges in international ventures and work arrangements. Covers recruitment, selection, training, performance management, and compensation in global settings, plus comparative labor relations and virtual teams.

MGT.name: Managing the Nonprofit Organization
MGT.description: Focuses on nonprofit development, management, and leadership. Students assess nonprofit excellence theories, accountability, funding, and sustainability.

MGT.name: Compensation Management
MGT.description: Provides understanding of effective, equitable compensation programs and how compensation supports operational and strategic objectives. Reviews plan objectives, implementation techniques, and federal compliance requirements.

MGT.name: Cross-Cultural Management
MGT.description: Emphasizes cultural, organizational, and management aspects of international business, focusing on leadership and motivation in a cross-cultural environment.

MGT.name: Strategic Management of Technological Innovation
MGT.description: Provides strategy framework for high-technology and startup companies. Teaches students to examine technical and managerial opportunities in emerging technologies, analyze organizational structures, and develop strategies for commercializing technology-based products and services.

MGT.name: Management Internship
MGT.description: Individually supervised employment in management areas (human resources, operations, general management) requiring application of management theory and principles.

MGT.name: Multinational Business Simulation
MGT.description: Semester-long computer simulation where teams manage competing multinational firms. Emphasizes finance, marketing, and production decisions requiring mastery of enterprise operations.

MGT.name: The Design Thinking Process
MGT.description: Hands-on course teaching design thinking process application while developing understanding of psychological principles underlying innovation, problem-solving, and gamification.

MGT.name: Internship: Human Resources Administration
MGT.description: Supervised internship applying human resource management principles requiring minimum ten hours weekly.

MGT.name: Human Resources Development
MGT.description: Examines four HRD main components: training/individual development, performance management, organization development, and career development. Explores needs analysis, learning acquisition and transfer, evaluation, and HRD practitioner competencies.

MGT.name: Human Resource Metrics and Analytics
MGT.description: Covers data analytics concepts and applications within HRM domain. Progresses from simplified HR issue illustration through functional aspects (workforce utilization, recruitment, engagement, talent development) to higher analytics maturity with data analysis and predictive recommendations.

MGT.name: Cases in Global Business Management
MGT.description: Expands knowledge from MGT 356 focusing on strategy implementation and effective operation in different global environmental and institutional settings.

MGT.name: Project Management I
MGT.description: Focuses on project management tools and techniques for managing complex, non-routine, one-time endeavors. Topics include scheduling, budgeting, cost control, team building and risk management.

MGT.name: Power and Influence
MGT.description: Explores power sources and influence principles in modern organizations. Evaluates strategies for employing power effectively, emphasizing influence without formal authority. Analyzes ethical issues and develops responsible stewardship approaches.

MGT.name: Employment Relations
MGT.description: Begins with historic American labor movement impact on workplace conflict resolution, then examines employment relations management, employee rights/responsibilities, labor relations, collective bargaining, and federal workplace laws.

MGT.name: Management Seminar
MGT.description: Students identify and understand trends in sociological, technological, and managerial environments for twenty-first century management. Develops personal management philosophies and styles.

MGT.name: Team Building and Conflict Resolution
MGT.description: Develops understanding of organizational conflict origins and effective management. Empowers students with skills and strategies for becoming effective team members and leaders.

MGT.name: Women and Leadership Strategies for Success and Professional Development
MGT.description: Focuses on women's organizational roles through professional development. Provides skill and competency acquisition in networking, negotiation, personal branding, leadership and career development.

MGT.name: Strategic Human Resource Management (SHRM)
MGT.description: HR capstone course integrating entire HR knowledge body within global and strategic frameworks.

MGT.name: Leadership Seminar
MGT.description: Addresses modern leadership challenges from changing social trends, technology, politics, and globalization. Studies current leadership theory through interactive case studies, exercises, film, and collaborative projects.

MGT.name: Project Management II
MGT.description: Builds on MGT 462 tools and techniques for managing organizational change. Students prepare project reports for companies. Prepares students for Associate Certification in Project Management Exam.

MGT.name: Directed Study in Management
MGT.description: Under faculty supervision, students pursue a well-defined management interest area.

marketing_mix_4ps.title: Marketing Mix (4 Ps)
marketing_mix_4ps.definition: A foundational marketing framework comprising four key decision areas that marketers control to meet customer needs and achieve organizational objectives.

key_terms.Product: Goods, services, or ideas offered to satisfy customer needs. Includes features, quality, design, branding, packaging, warranties, and product line decisions.
key_terms.Price: The amount charged for a product. Includes list price, discounts, allowances, payment terms, and credit terms. Must reflect value to customer and cover costs.
key_terms.Place (Distribution): Activities that make the product available to target consumers. Includes channels, coverage, locations, inventory, transportation, and logistics.
key_terms.Promotion: Activities that communicate product merits and persuade target customers to buy. Includes advertising, personal selling, sales promotion, public relations, and direct marketing.

extended_mix_7ps.People: All human actors who play a role in service delivery and influence buyer perceptions (employees, customers).
extended_mix_7ps.Process: The procedures, mechanisms, and flow of activities by which services are consumed.
extended_mix_7ps.Physical Evidence: The environment in which the service is delivered, including tangible components that facilitate performance.

real_world_examples: Apple iPhone: Premium product design (Product), premium pricing strategy (Price), Apple Stores and authorized retailers (Place), iconic advertising campaigns (Promotion)

Starbucks: Customized beverages (Product), value-based pricing (Price), ubiquitous store locations (Place), loyalty program and social media (Promotion), barista training (People), order process (Process), store ambiance (Physical Evidence)
common_exam_topics: Identify which P a given business decision falls under
Explain how 4Ps interact and must be consistent
Compare 4Ps to 7Ps and when to use each
Apply the marketing mix to a case study

stp_process.title: Market Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning (STP)
stp_process.definition: A three-step strategic marketing process: (1) divide the market into distinct groups, (2) evaluate and select the most attractive segments, (3) develop a competitive position for the product in the minds of target customers.

key_terms.Market Segmentation: Dividing a market into distinct subsets of consumers with common needs or characteristics.
key_terms.Segmentation Bases: Geographic (region, city size, climate), Demographic (age, gender, income, education), Psychographic (lifestyle, personality, values via VALS), Behavioral (usage rate, loyalty, benefits sought, occasion).
key_terms.Positioning: Designing a product's offering and image to occupy a distinctive place in the target market's mind.
key_terms.Positioning Statement: For [target segment], [brand] is the [frame of reference] that [point of difference] because [reason to believe].
key_terms.Perceptual Map: A visual tool plotting brands on axes representing key attributes as perceived by consumers.
key_terms.Value Proposition: The full mix of benefits upon which a brand is positioned.

real_world_examples: Nike: Segments by sport/activity and lifestyle; targets serious athletes and aspirational fitness consumers; positions as performance and inspiration ('Just Do It')
Toyota: Multiple segments with different brands (Lexus for luxury, Toyota for reliability, Scion for youth); each positioned differently on perceptual maps
common_exam_topics: Identify segmentation bases in a scenario
Evaluate segment attractiveness (measurable, accessible, substantial, differentiable, actionable)
Construct a positioning statement
Read and interpret a perceptual map

consumer_behavior.title: Consumer Behavior and Decision-Making Process
consumer_behavior.definition: The study of how individuals, groups, and organizations select, buy, use, and dispose of goods, services, ideas, or experiences to satisfy needs and desires.

key_terms.Consumer Decision-Making Process: Five stages: (1) Need/Problem Recognition, (2) Information Search, (3) Evaluation of Alternatives, (4) Purchase Decision, (5) Post-Purchase Behavior.
key_terms.Involvement: Level of personal relevance or importance of a purchase. High involvement = extensive problem solving; Low involvement = habitual/routine.
key_terms.Perception: Process of selecting, organizing, and interpreting stimuli. Includes selective attention, distortion, and retention.
key_terms.Motivation: Internal drive. Maslow's hierarchy applied: physiological, safety, social, esteem, self-actualization needs drive purchases.
key_terms.Attitudes: Learned predispositions to behave favorably or unfavorably. ABC model: Affect, Behavior, Cognition.
key_terms.Reference Groups: Groups that serve as frames of reference for individuals in purchase decisions (aspirational, membership, dissociative).
key_terms.Culture and Subculture: Broadest influence on behavior. Includes values, beliefs, customs passed through generations.
key_terms.Cognitive Dissonance: Post-purchase doubt about whether the right decision was made, especially for high-involvement purchases.
key_terms.Evoked Set: The set of brands a consumer actually considers before making a purchase.

real_world_examples: Car buying: High involvement, extensive information search (online reviews, dealer visits), evaluate alternatives (safety, price, features), post-purchase rationalization
Grocery shopping: Low involvement, habitual decision-making, brand loyalty, influenced by in-store promotions and shelf placement
common_exam_topics: Trace through the 5-stage decision process for a scenario
Explain high vs low involvement purchasing
Identify cultural, social, personal, and psychological influences
Apply cognitive dissonance to a post-purchase situation

marketing_research.title: Marketing Research Methods
marketing_research.definition: The systematic design, collection, analysis, and reporting of data and findings relevant to a specific marketing situation facing a company.

key_terms.Research Process: Define the problem > Develop research plan > Collect data > Analyze data > Present findings.
key_terms.Primary Data: Data collected firsthand for the specific research purpose. Methods: surveys, experiments, observation, focus groups, interviews.
key_terms.Secondary Data: Data that already exists, collected for another purpose. Sources: government data, industry reports, company records, academic journals.
key_terms.Qualitative Research: Exploratory, non-numerical. Focus groups, depth interviews, ethnography, projective techniques.
key_terms.Quantitative Research: Structured, numerical. Surveys, experiments, statistical analysis.
key_terms.Sampling: Probability (random, stratified, cluster) vs Non-probability (convenience, judgment, snowball, quota).
key_terms.Survey Methods: Online, telephone, mail, personal interview. Each has trade-offs in cost, speed, response rate, and bias.
key_terms.Experimental Design: Testing cause-and-effect by manipulating independent variables and measuring dependent variables. A/B testing is a common form.
key_terms.Validity: Whether the research measures what it intends to measure.

frameworks: Exploratory vs Descriptive vs Causal research designs
real_world_examples: Netflix uses A/B testing (experimental research) on thumbnails and UI elements to optimize engagement
Procter & Gamble conducts extensive focus groups (qualitative) before launching new consumer products
common_exam_topics: Distinguish primary vs secondary data with examples
Choose appropriate research design for a scenario
Identify sampling method strengths/weaknesses
Explain reliability vs validity

branding_brand_equity.title: Branding and Brand Equity
branding_brand_equity.definition: A brand is a name, term, sign, symbol, design, or combination that identifies and differentiates a seller's products. Brand equity is the added value endowed by the brand name.

key_terms.Brand Equity: The differential effect that knowing the brand name has on customer response to the product. Keller's model: brand salience, performance, imagery, judgments, feelings, resonance.
key_terms.Brand Awareness: Ability to recognize or recall a brand. Includes recognition (aided) and recall (unaided).
key_terms.Brand Loyalty: The degree to which consumers consistently purchase the same brand. Levels: switchers, habitual, satisfied, brand likers, committed.
key_terms.Brand Associations: Anything linked in memory to a brand: attributes, benefits, attitudes.
key_terms.Brand Architecture: How brands within a company portfolio relate. Types: branded house (Google), house of brands (P&G), endorsed (Marriott Courtyard), sub-brands.
key_terms.Brand Extension: Using an existing brand name to launch a product in a new category. Risk: dilution.
key_terms.Co-Branding: Two brands featured on a single product (e.g., Nike + Apple).
key_terms.Brand Positioning: How a brand is perceived relative to competitors in the minds of consumers.

real_world_examples: Coca-Cola: High brand equity built over 130+ years; brand value estimated at $90B+; consistent positioning around happiness and refreshment
Amazon: Brand trust and convenience drive equity; extended from books to everything; Prime membership builds loyalty
common_exam_topics: Explain Keller's CBBE pyramid levels
Evaluate brand extension decisions
Distinguish brand awareness, image, and loyalty
Calculate/discuss brand equity implications

digital_marketing.title: Digital Marketing and Social Media Marketing
digital_marketing.definition: Marketing efforts that use electronic devices or the internet. Encompasses all marketing efforts using digital channels to connect with current and prospective customers.

key_terms.SEO (Search Engine Optimization): Optimizing website content to rank higher in organic search results. On-page (content, keywords, meta tags) and off-page (backlinks, domain authority).
key_terms.SEM (Search Engine Marketing): Paid search advertising (Google Ads/PPC). Bid on keywords; pay per click.
key_terms.Content Marketing: Creating and distributing valuable, relevant content to attract and retain a defined audience.
key_terms.Social Media Marketing: Using platforms (Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, X, Facebook) to promote products and engage audiences. Organic vs paid social.
key_terms.Email Marketing: Direct communication via email. Metrics: open rate, click-through rate, conversion rate, unsubscribe rate.
key_terms.Influencer Marketing: Partnering with individuals who have significant social media followings to promote products.
key_terms.Marketing Automation: Software to automate repetitive marketing tasks (email sequences, social posting, lead nurturing).
key_terms.Conversion Rate: Percentage of visitors who complete a desired action (purchase, sign-up).
key_terms.Customer Journey Mapping: Visualizing the complete experience a customer has with a brand across all digital and physical touchpoints.
key_terms.KPIs: Key performance indicators: CTR, CPC, CPM, ROAS, CAC, CLV.

real_world_examples: Dollar Shave Club: Viral video content marketing launched a $1B brand acquired by Unilever
Glossier: Built entirely through social media and user-generated content; community-first digital strategy
common_exam_topics: Compare SEO vs SEM
Explain the digital marketing funnel
Calculate basic digital metrics (CTR, conversion rate)
Design a social media strategy for a brand

b2b_vs_b2c.title: B2B vs B2C Marketing
b2b_vs_b2c.definition: B2B (Business-to-Business) marketing involves selling products/services to other businesses. B2C (Business-to-Consumer) marketing targets individual end consumers.

key_terms.B2B Characteristics: Fewer but larger buyers, derived demand, inelastic demand, professional purchasing, longer sales cycles, relationship-focused, buying center (DMU).
key_terms.B2C Characteristics: Many individual buyers, direct demand, emotional and impulsive buying, shorter sales cycles, brand-driven.
key_terms.Buying Center (DMU): Decision Making Unit in B2B: Initiators, Users, Influencers, Deciders, Approvers, Buyers, Gatekeepers.
key_terms.Derived Demand: B2B demand that derives from demand for consumer goods.
key_terms.Supply Chain Management: Coordinating the flow of materials, information, and finances from supplier to manufacturer to wholesaler to retailer to consumer.

Marketing_channels.B2B: Trade shows, LinkedIn, direct sales, industry publications
Marketing_channels.B2C: Mass media, social media, retail, e-commerce

real_world_examples: Salesforce (B2B): Sells CRM software to businesses; long sales cycles; relationship selling; ROI-focused messaging
Coca-Cola (B2C): Sells beverages to consumers; emotional branding; mass advertising; impulse purchase
common_exam_topics: Compare B2B and B2C on 5+ dimensions
Identify buying center roles in a scenario
Explain derived demand with an example

product_life_cycle.title: Product Life Cycle (PLC)
product_life_cycle.definition: The course of a product's sales and profits over its lifetime, typically described in four stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline.

key_terms.Introduction Stage: Product launched. Low sales, high costs per customer, negative profits. Goal: create awareness and trial. Promotion-heavy, limited distribution.
key_terms.Growth Stage: Sales climb rapidly. Rising profits, increasing competition. Goal: maximize market share. Expand distribution, improve product.
key_terms.Maturity Stage: Sales peak then plateau. Profits stabilize or decline. Intense competition, price pressure. Goal: defend share, increase profits. Differentiate, find new segments.
key_terms.Decline Stage: Sales and profits drop. Goal: reduce expenditure, harvest or divest. Options: maintain, harvest, or drop the product.
key_terms.Adoption Curve: Rogers' Diffusion of Innovations: Innovators (2.5%), Early Adopters (13.5%), Early Majority (34%), Late Majority (34%), Laggards (16%).

real_world_examples: Smartphones: Introduction (2007 iPhone), Growth (2008-2015), Maturity (2016-present, saturated market, incremental innovation)
DVD players: Full cycle from introduction through decline; replaced by streaming
common_exam_topics: Identify PLC stage from sales/profit data
Recommend marketing strategies for each stage
Relate adoption curve to PLC
Explain how to extend the maturity stage

pricing_strategies.title: Pricing Strategies
pricing_strategies.definition: Methods companies use to price their products or services, balancing cost recovery, value delivery, competitive positioning, and profit objectives.

key_terms.Cost-Based Pricing: Setting price based on costs plus a markup (cost-plus pricing). Simple but ignores demand and competition.
key_terms.Value-Based Pricing: Setting price based on perceived customer value rather than cost. Requires understanding customer willingness to pay.
key_terms.Competition-Based Pricing: Setting prices based on competitors' strategies. Going rate, above-market, below-market.
key_terms.Price Skimming: Setting high initial price to 'skim' revenue from early adopters, then lowering over time. Works with innovative products.
key_terms.Penetration Pricing: Setting low initial price to gain market share quickly. Works in price-sensitive markets.
key_terms.Dynamic Pricing: Adjusting prices in real-time based on demand, time, customer segment (airlines, Uber surge pricing).
key_terms.Psychological Pricing: Pricing that considers psychology: odd pricing ($9.99), prestige pricing, reference pricing, bundle pricing.
key_terms.Price Elasticity of Demand: Measure of how sensitive quantity demanded is to a price change. Elastic (>1): sensitive; Inelastic (<1): insensitive.
key_terms.Freemium: Basic product free, premium features paid. Common in SaaS and apps.
key_terms.Loss Leader: Pricing a product below cost to attract customers who then purchase other profitable items.

real_world_examples: Apple: Price skimming on new iPhone models; prestige pricing reinforces premium brand
Walmart: Everyday low pricing (EDLP) strategy; penetration pricing for new markets
Spotify: Freemium model; free tier with ads, premium subscription without
common_exam_topics: Calculate break-even point
Recommend pricing strategy for a new product scenario
Explain skimming vs penetration with conditions for each
Calculate price elasticity and interpret

distribution_channels.title: Distribution Channels
distribution_channels.definition: The set of interdependent organizations involved in the process of making a product or service available for use or consumption by the consumer or business user.

key_terms.Channel Levels: Direct (manufacturer to consumer, 0-level), Indirect (1+ intermediaries: retailer, wholesaler, agent/broker).
key_terms.Channel Functions: Information, promotion, negotiation, ordering, financing, risk taking, physical possession, payment, title transfer.
key_terms.Intensive Distribution: Product available in as many outlets as possible (convenience goods: Coca-Cola).
key_terms.Selective Distribution: Limited number of outlets in a given area (shopping goods: appliances).
key_terms.Exclusive Distribution: Very limited outlets; often one per territory (specialty goods: luxury brands, car dealerships).
key_terms.Vertical Marketing System (VMS): Unified channel system. Types: Corporate (single ownership), Contractual (franchises), Administered (dominant member coordinates).
key_terms.Omnichannel: Integrated approach across all channels (physical stores, online, mobile, social) providing seamless customer experience.
key_terms.Channel Conflict: Disagreements between channel members over goals, roles, or rewards. Horizontal vs vertical conflict.
key_terms.Disintermediation: Removal of intermediaries (e.g., manufacturers selling direct online).

frameworks: Channel design decisions (analyze needs, set objectives, identify alternatives, evaluate)
real_world_examples: Tesla: Direct-to-consumer model, no dealerships; disintermediation
Nike: Shifted from wholesale-heavy to DTC (Nike.com, Nike stores) while maintaining selective retail partners
common_exam_topics: Identify distribution intensity for a product type
Explain channel conflict with an example
Compare push vs pull distribution strategies
Design a channel strategy for a given product

integrated_marketing_communications.title: Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC)
integrated_marketing_communications.definition: A strategic approach that coordinates all promotional tools and messages to deliver a consistent, unified message across all customer touchpoints.

key_terms.Promotion Mix Elements: Advertising, Sales Promotion, Personal Selling, Public Relations, Direct Marketing, Digital/Social Media.
key_terms.Advertising: Paid, non-personal communication through mass media. Types: informative, persuasive, reminder.
key_terms.Sales Promotion: Short-term incentives to encourage purchase. Consumer (coupons, samples, contests) and Trade (allowances, co-op advertising).
key_terms.Personal Selling: Face-to-face interaction with prospective buyers. Most effective for B2B and high-involvement purchases.
key_terms.Public Relations: Building good relations with publics through favorable publicity, corporate image, and handling unfavorable events. Tools: press releases, events, sponsorships.
key_terms.Direct Marketing: Direct connections with targeted consumers (email, direct mail, catalogs, telemarketing).
key_terms.AIDA Model: Attention > Interest > Desire > Action. Framework for designing promotional messages.
key_terms.Media Planning: Selecting the right media mix (TV, digital, print, outdoor) based on reach, frequency, impact, and cost.
key_terms.CPM: Cost Per Thousand impressions. Standard metric for comparing media costs.

frameworks: DAGMAR (Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results)
real_world_examples: Dove 'Real Beauty' campaign: Integrated TV ads, social media, PR, in-store displays with consistent body-positivity message across all channels
Red Bull: IMC through extreme sports sponsorship (PR), content marketing, social media, and experiential events all reinforcing 'gives you wings' positioning
common_exam_topics: Explain why IMC is important (consistency, efficiency)
Apply AIDA to a promotional message
Choose appropriate promotion mix for B2B vs B2C
Calculate CPM and compare media options

swot_competitive_analysis.title: SWOT Analysis and Competitive Analysis
swot_competitive_analysis.definition: SWOT is a framework for identifying internal Strengths and Weaknesses and external Opportunities and Threats. Competitive analysis is the systematic evaluation of competitors' strategies, capabilities, and market positions.

key_terms.Strengths: Internal capabilities that give advantage (brand, patents, skilled workforce, efficient operations).
key_terms.Weaknesses: Internal limitations that create disadvantage (high costs, outdated technology, weak brand, limited distribution).
key_terms.Opportunities: External factors the company could exploit (market growth, new technology, regulatory changes, demographic shifts).
key_terms.Threats: External factors that could cause trouble (new competitors, economic downturn, changing consumer preferences, regulation).
key_terms.Competitive Advantage: A condition that allows a company to produce goods/services better or more cheaply than rivals. Sustainable when not easily copied.
key_terms.Competitor Analysis Framework: Identify competitors > Assess objectives/strategies/strengths/weaknesses > Select competitors to attack or avoid.
key_terms.Porter's Five Forces: (Also in management) Threat of new entrants, Bargaining power of suppliers, Bargaining power of buyers, Threat of substitutes, Rivalry among existing competitors.
key_terms.PESTEL Analysis: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal external factors analysis.

real_world_examples: Netflix SWOT: Strengths (brand, content library, data analytics), Weaknesses (high content costs, debt), Opportunities (global expansion, gaming), Threats (Disney+, password sharing crackdowns)

Porter's Five Forces for airline industry: High rivalry, moderate supplier power (Boeing/Airbus duopoly), high buyer power (price sensitivity), high threat of substitutes (trains, video conferencing), moderate entry barriers
common_exam_topics: Conduct a SWOT analysis for a given company
Apply Porter's Five Forces to an industry
Distinguish internal vs external factors
Use TOWS to generate strategy recommendations

management_functions.title: Management Functions (POLC)
management_functions.definition: The four fundamental functions of management that all managers perform: Planning, Organizing, Leading, and Controlling.

key_terms.Planning: Setting organizational goals and determining the best course of action. Includes strategic planning (long-term, top management), tactical planning (medium-term, middle management), and operational planning (short-term, first-line management).
key_terms.Organizing: Arranging resources and tasks to achieve organizational goals. Includes designing organizational structure, delegating authority, establishing reporting relationships.
key_terms.Leading: Directing and motivating employees to achieve organizational goals. Includes communication, motivation, leadership style, and conflict resolution.
key_terms.Controlling: Monitoring performance, comparing to standards, and taking corrective action. Includes setting standards, measuring performance, comparing, correcting.
key_terms.Management Levels: Top (CEO, VP: strategic), Middle (department heads: tactical), First-line (supervisors: operational).
key_terms.Mintzberg's Managerial Roles: Interpersonal (figurehead, leader, liaison), Informational (monitor, disseminator, spokesperson), Decisional (entrepreneur, disturbance handler, resource allocator, negotiator).
key_terms.Efficiency vs Effectiveness: Efficiency = doing things right (minimize resources); Effectiveness = doing the right things (achieve goals).

real_world_examples: Planning: Amazon's long-term strategy to dominate e-commerce, cloud computing, and AI
Organizing: Google's matrix organizational structure balancing product teams and functional departments
Leading: Satya Nadella transforming Microsoft's culture from 'know-it-all' to 'learn-it-all'
Controlling: Toyota's quality control systems and continuous improvement (kaizen)
common_exam_topics: Identify which function a manager is performing in a scenario
Explain Fayol's principles with modern examples
Distinguish strategic, tactical, and operational planning
Describe the control process steps

organizational_behavior_motivation.title: Organizational Behavior and Motivation Theories
organizational_behavior_motivation.definition: OB is the study of how people behave in organizational settings. Motivation theories explain what drives individuals to act and sustain effort toward goals.

key_terms.Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs: Five levels (bottom to top): Physiological, Safety, Social/Belonging, Esteem, Self-Actualization. Lower needs must be substantially met before higher needs motivate.
key_terms.Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory: Hygiene factors (salary, job security, working conditions) prevent dissatisfaction but don't motivate. Motivators (achievement, recognition, responsibility, growth) create satisfaction and motivation.
key_terms.McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y: Theory X: managers assume workers are lazy, need coercion and control. Theory Y: managers assume workers are self-motivated, seek responsibility, and are creative.
key_terms.Expectancy Theory (Vroom): Motivation = Expectancy (effort leads to performance) x Instrumentality (performance leads to reward) x Valence (value of reward).
key_terms.Equity Theory (Adams): People compare their input/output ratio to others. Perceived inequity leads to motivation to restore equity (reduce effort, seek raise, leave).
key_terms.Goal-Setting Theory (Locke): Specific, difficult but achievable goals with feedback lead to higher performance than vague or easy goals.
key_terms.Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan): Three innate needs: Autonomy, Competence, Relatedness. Intrinsic motivation strongest when all three are met.
key_terms.Reinforcement Theory (Skinner): Behavior shaped by consequences: positive reinforcement, negative reinforcement, punishment, extinction.
key_terms.Job Characteristics Model (Hackman & Old: Five core dimensions (skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, feedback) lead to critical psychological states and outcomes (motivation, satisfaction, performance).

real_world_examples: Maslow: Google provides free meals (physiological), job security (safety), collaborative spaces (social), peer recognition programs (esteem), 20% time for personal projects (self-actualization)
Herzberg: A pay raise (hygiene) stops complaints but doesn't motivate; assigning a challenging project (motivator) drives engagement
Theory Y: W.L. Gore (Gore-Tex) uses flat structure, self-managed teams, assumes employees are creative and responsible
Equity Theory: Employee discovers a peer with same role earns 20% more; reduces effort or negotiates raise
common_exam_topics: Compare and contrast Maslow, Herzberg, and McGregor
Apply Vroom's expectancy theory to a scenario
Identify hygiene vs motivator factors
Explain how equity theory predicts employee reactions
Apply goal-setting theory principles to improve performance

leadership_styles_theories.title: Leadership Styles and Theories
leadership_styles_theories.definition: Leadership is the ability to influence a group toward the achievement of goals. Theories explain where leadership comes from and how it can be practiced effectively.

key_terms.Trait Theory: Leaders possess certain inherent traits (intelligence, self-confidence, determination, integrity, sociability). Criticisms: doesn't account for situations, not predictive.
key_terms.Behavioral Theories: Focus on what leaders do, not who they are. Ohio State studies: Initiating Structure (task) vs Consideration (relationship). Michigan studies: Production-oriented vs Employee-oriented.

Situational/Contingency Theories.Fiedler's Contingency Model: Leadership effectiveness depends on match between leader style (task vs relationship) and situational favorableness (leader-member relations, task structure, position power).
Situational/Contingency Theories.Hersey-Blanchard Situational Leadership: Leader adapts style (Telling, Selling, Participating, Delegating) based on follower readiness/maturity.
Situational/Contingency Theories.Path-Goal Theory (House): Leader's job is to assist followers in attaining goals. Four styles: Directive, Supportive, Participative, Achievement-oriented.

key_terms.Transformational Leadership: Inspires followers to transcend self-interest. Four I's: Idealized Influence, Inspirational Motivation, Intellectual Stimulation, Individualized Consideration.
key_terms.Transactional Leadership: Exchange-based: rewards for performance, management by exception (active/passive).
key_terms.Servant Leadership: Leader's primary role is to serve followers. Emphasizes empathy, listening, stewardship, community building.
key_terms.Authentic Leadership: Self-aware, transparent, ethical, balanced processing of information.
key_terms.Autocratic vs Democratic vs Laissez-fair: Autocratic (leader decides alone), Democratic (leader involves group), Laissez-faire (leader hands off).

real_world_examples: Transformational: Steve Jobs inspired Apple employees with a vision of changing the world through technology
Servant: Howard Schultz at Starbucks prioritized employee benefits (health insurance for part-timers) and called employees 'partners'
Situational: A new employee (low readiness) needs directive/telling style; an experienced self-starter needs delegating style
common_exam_topics: Match leadership style to a scenario using situational leadership
Compare transformational vs transactional leadership
Identify leadership style from a case study
Explain why no single style works in all situations
Apply path-goal theory to a workplace scenario

organizational_structure_design.title: Organizational Structure and Design
organizational_structure_design.definition: The formal system of task and authority relationships that controls how people coordinate their actions and use resources to achieve organizational goals.

key_terms.Organizational Chart: Visual representation of reporting relationships and structure.
key_terms.Chain of Command: Unbroken line of authority extending from top to bottom. Unity of command: each person reports to one manager.
key_terms.Span of Control: Number of subordinates a manager can effectively manage. Wide (many subordinates, flat org) vs Narrow (few subordinates, tall org).
key_terms.Centralization vs Decentralization: Centralized: decisions made at top. Decentralized: decision authority pushed to lower levels.
key_terms.Departmentalization: Grouping by function, product, geography, customer, or process.
key_terms.Functional Structure: Grouped by business function (marketing, finance, operations). Efficient but can create silos.
key_terms.Divisional Structure: Grouped by product line, geography, or customer type. More responsive but duplicates resources.
key_terms.Matrix Structure: Dual reporting relationships (functional and product/project). Flexible but can create confusion and conflict.
key_terms.Flat vs Tall Organizations: Flat: few levels, wide span. Tall: many levels, narrow span.
key_terms.Mechanistic vs Organic: Mechanistic: rigid, formal, centralized (stable environments). Organic: flexible, informal, decentralized (dynamic environments).
key_terms.Formalization: Degree to which jobs are standardized with rules and procedures.

key_theorists: Tom Burns & G.M. Stalker (mechanistic vs organic)
real_world_examples: Functional: A small manufacturing company with marketing, finance, HR, and production departments
Divisional: Johnson & Johnson operates as a collection of independent companies organized by product line
Matrix: NASA uses matrix structure where engineers report to both functional departments and project managers
Flat: Valve Corporation (game developer) has no formal management hierarchy
common_exam_topics: Identify structure type from an org chart
Recommend a structure for a given company scenario
Explain trade-offs of centralization vs decentralization
Compare mechanistic vs organic structures and when each is appropriate

strategic_management.title: Strategic Management
strategic_management.definition: The set of managerial decisions and actions that determines the long-run performance of an organization. Includes environmental scanning, strategy formulation, strategy implementation, and evaluation/control.

key_terms.Mission Statement: Declares the organization's reason for being and scope of operations.
key_terms.Vision Statement: Describes what the organization aspires to become.
key_terms.Porter's Five Forces: Industry analysis framework: (1) Threat of new entrants (barriers to entry), (2) Bargaining power of suppliers, (3) Bargaining power of buyers, (4) Threat of substitutes, (5) Rivalry among existing competitors.
key_terms.Porter's Generic Strategies: Cost Leadership (low cost, broad market), Differentiation (unique product, broad market), Focus (cost or differentiation in narrow segment). Stuck in the middle: danger of not committing to a strategy.
key_terms.Value Chain Analysis (Porter): Primary activities (inbound logistics, operations, outbound logistics, marketing/sales, service) and Support activities (firm infrastructure, HRM, technology development, procurement). Identify where value is created or costs can be reduced.
key_terms.BCG Matrix: Portfolio analysis: Stars (high growth, high share), Cash Cows (low growth, high share), Question Marks (high growth, low share), Dogs (low growth, low share).
key_terms.Ansoff Matrix: Growth strategies: Market Penetration, Market Development, Product Development, Diversification.
key_terms.Core Competency: A unique set of capabilities that gives a company competitive advantage (Prahalad & Hamel).
key_terms.Balanced Scorecard: Performance measurement across four perspectives: Financial, Customer, Internal Processes, Learning & Growth (Kaplan & Norton).

real_world_examples: Porter's Five Forces for Coffee Industry: High rivalry (Starbucks, Dunkin', local shops), Low supplier power (many coffee growers), Moderate buyer power, Low substitute threat, Low entry barriers for local shops but high for national chains
Cost Leadership: Walmart's supply chain efficiency enables everyday low prices
Differentiation: Apple differentiates through design, ecosystem, and brand experience
Value Chain: Zara's value chain advantage is in rapid design-to-store operations (2 weeks vs industry 6 months)
common_exam_topics: Apply Porter's Five Forces to a given industry
Identify which generic strategy a company uses
Conduct a value chain analysis
Use BCG matrix to recommend portfolio strategies
Distinguish mission from vision statements

human_resource_management.title: Human Resource Management Basics
human_resource_management.definition: The process of acquiring, training, appraising, and compensating employees, and attending to their labor relations, health and safety, and fairness concerns.

key_terms.HR Planning: Forecasting future HR needs and ensuring the right number of qualified people are in the right jobs at the right time.
key_terms.Job Analysis: Systematic process of collecting information about job duties, responsibilities, and requirements. Produces job descriptions and job specifications.
key_terms.Recruitment: Process of generating a pool of qualified applicants. Internal (transfers, promotions) vs External (job boards, agencies, campus recruiting).
key_terms.Selection: Choosing from applicants. Tools: interviews (structured/unstructured), tests (aptitude, personality), assessment centers, background checks.
key_terms.Training and Development: Training: improving current job skills. Development: preparing for future roles. Methods: on-the-job, mentoring, classroom, e-learning, simulation.
key_terms.Performance Appraisal: Evaluating employee performance against standards. Methods: 360-degree feedback, MBO, graphic rating scales, critical incident, BARS (Behaviorally Anchored Rating Scales).
key_terms.Compensation: Direct (salary, wages, bonuses) and Indirect (benefits: health insurance, retirement plans, PTO).
key_terms.Employment Law: Title VII (no discrimination based on race, sex, religion, national origin, color), ADA (disability accommodation), ADEA (age), FMLA (family leave), FLSA (minimum wage, overtime), OSHA (workplace safety).
key_terms.At-Will Employment: Employer or employee can terminate the relationship at any time for any lawful reason.

real_world_examples: Google's hiring: Structured interviews, work sample tests, cross-functional hiring committees reduce bias
Costco: Higher wages and better benefits than competitors result in lower turnover and higher productivity
common_exam_topics: Describe the recruitment and selection process
Compare performance appraisal methods
Identify which employment law applies to a scenario
Explain strategic HR alignment with business strategy

operations_management.title: Operations Management
operations_management.definition: The administration of business practices to create the highest level of efficiency possible within an organization, concerned with converting materials and labor into goods and services as efficiently as possible.

key_terms.Operations Strategy: How the operations function contributes to competitive strategy through decisions about process, quality, capacity, and inventory.
key_terms.Process Design: Layout and flow of production. Types: Job shop (custom, low volume), Batch, Assembly line (high volume, standardized), Continuous flow.

Quality Management.TQM (Total Quality Management): Organization-wide approach to continuous quality improvement in all processes.
Quality Management.Six Sigma: Data-driven methodology to eliminate defects (DMAIC: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control). 3.4 defects per million opportunities.

key_terms.Lean Operations: Minimizing waste (muda) while maximizing value. Tools: Just-In-Time (JIT), Kanban, 5S, Value Stream Mapping, Kaizen (continuous improvement).
key_terms.Inventory Management: EOQ (Economic Order Quantity), JIT, ABC analysis, Safety stock. Balancing holding costs vs ordering costs vs stockout costs.
key_terms.Supply Chain Management: Coordinating all activities from raw materials to final customer delivery. Includes supplier selection, logistics, warehousing, demand forecasting.
key_terms.Capacity Planning: Determining the production capacity needed to meet changing demands.
key_terms.Project Management: Planning and controlling one-time projects. Tools: Gantt charts, CPM (Critical Path Method), PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique).
key_terms.Forecasting: Predicting future demand. Qualitative (expert opinion, Delphi method) and Quantitative (moving averages, exponential smoothing, regression).

key_theorists: W. Edwards Deming (14 Points, PDCA cycle)
real_world_examples: Toyota: Pioneer of lean manufacturing (TPS), JIT inventory, kaizen culture
Amazon: Operations excellence in fulfillment center design, robotics, last-mile delivery optimization
Six Sigma at Motorola: Originated Six Sigma methodology; later adopted and popularized by GE under Jack Welch
common_exam_topics: Calculate EOQ
Explain TQM principles
Compare lean vs Six Sigma
Identify process type for a given production scenario
Create/analyze a Gantt chart or critical path

change_management.title: Change Management
change_management.definition: The process, tools, and techniques to manage the people side of change to achieve a required business outcome.

key_terms.Lewin's Change Model: Three stages: Unfreeze (create urgency, challenge status quo), Change/Move (implement new processes, behaviors), Refreeze (stabilize and reinforce the change).
key_terms.Kotter's 8-Step Model: (1) Create urgency, (2) Form a guiding coalition, (3) Create a vision, (4) Communicate the vision, (5) Empower broad-based action, (6) Generate short-term wins, (7) Consolidate gains, (8) Anchor in culture.
key_terms.Resistance to Change: Common reasons: fear of unknown, loss of control, bad timing, peer pressure, self-interest, misunderstanding, lack of trust.
key_terms.Overcoming Resistance: Education/communication, participation, facilitation/support, negotiation, manipulation/co-optation, coercion (Kotter & Schlesinger).
key_terms.Organizational Development (OD): Planned, systematic process of change using behavioral science knowledge. Interventions: team building, survey feedback, process consultation.
key_terms.Force Field Analysis (Lewin): Identifies driving forces (pushing for change) and restraining forces (resisting change). Change occurs when driving forces exceed restraining forces.
key_terms.Planned vs Emergent Change: Planned: deliberate, structured. Emergent: evolves in response to the environment, less predictable.

real_world_examples: Kotter's 8 steps at Ford: Alan Mulally created urgency (near-bankruptcy), built coalition (leadership team), vision ('One Ford'), short-term wins (profitable quarters), anchored new culture
Resistance: Kodak's failure to transition from film to digital despite inventing the digital camera (resistance from profitable film business)
common_exam_topics: Apply Lewin's model to a change scenario
List Kotter's 8 steps in order
Identify sources of resistance and recommend solutions
Conduct a force field analysis

business_ethics_csr.title: Business Ethics and Corporate Social Responsibility
business_ethics_csr.definition: Business ethics refers to applying ethical principles to business situations. CSR is a company's commitment to managing its social, environmental, and economic effects responsibly and in line with stakeholder expectations.

key_terms.Ethics: Moral principles that guide behavior. Business context: honesty, fairness, integrity, respect for stakeholders.

Ethical Decision-Making Frameworks.Utilitarian: Greatest good for the greatest number (consequentialist).
Ethical Decision-Making Frameworks.Rights-Based: Respects fundamental rights of individuals regardless of outcomes.
Ethical Decision-Making Frameworks.Justice/Fairness: Fair distribution of benefits and burdens.
Ethical Decision-Making Frameworks.Virtue Ethics: Focus on character and moral virtues of the decision-maker.

key_terms.Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR): Carroll's Pyramid: (base) Economic responsibilities > Legal responsibilities > Ethical responsibilities > Philanthropic responsibilities (top).
key_terms.Stakeholder Theory (Freeman): Managers should consider all stakeholders (employees, customers, suppliers, community, shareholders, environment), not just shareholders.
key_terms.Shareholder Theory (Friedman): The social responsibility of business is to increase profits (within the law).
key_terms.Triple Bottom Line: People, Planet, Profit. Measuring success beyond financial performance.
key_terms.Whistleblowing: Reporting illegal or unethical practices within an organization. Protected under Sarbanes-Oxley and Dodd-Frank.
key_terms.Code of Ethics: Formal document stating an organization's values and ethical rules.
key_terms.ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance): Framework for evaluating corporate behavior beyond financial metrics.

real_world_examples: Patagonia: 'Don't buy this jacket' campaign; donates 1% of sales to environment; B Corp certified
Enron: Ethical failure - massive accounting fraud, destroyed shareholder value, led to Sarbanes-Oxley Act
Ben & Jerry's: Social mission integrated into business model; fair trade sourcing; community activism
common_exam_topics: Apply ethical frameworks to a business dilemma
Explain Carroll's CSR pyramid with examples
Compare stakeholder vs shareholder theory
Analyze a case study for ethical violations
Discuss the business case for CSR

team_dynamics_conflict.title: Team Dynamics and Conflict Resolution
team_dynamics_conflict.definition: Team dynamics refers to the behavioral relationships between group members that affect performance. Conflict resolution involves managing disagreements constructively.

key_terms.Tuckman's Stages of Group Development: Forming (orientation, getting to know each other), Storming (conflict, power struggles), Norming (establishing norms, cohesion develops), Performing (high performance, effective collaboration), Adjourning (dissolution, completion).
key_terms.Team Roles (Belbin): Nine roles: Plant (creative), Resource Investigator, Coordinator, Shaper, Monitor Evaluator, Teamworker, Implementer, Completer Finisher, Specialist.
key_terms.Groupthink (Janis): Desire for conformity overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives. Symptoms: illusion of invulnerability, collective rationalization, stereotyping outgroups, self-censorship.
key_terms.Social Loafing: Tendency for individuals to exert less effort when working collectively. Countered by smaller groups, individual accountability, meaningful tasks.
key_terms.Conflict Types: Task conflict (disagreements about work content - can be productive), Relationship conflict (personal friction - usually destructive), Process conflict (how work should be done).
key_terms.Thomas-Kilmann Conflict Modes: Competing (assertive, uncooperative), Collaborating (assertive, cooperative), Compromising (moderate), Avoiding (unassertive, uncooperative), Accommodating (unassertive, cooperative).
key_terms.Negotiation: Distributive (win-lose, fixed pie) vs Integrative (win-win, expand pie). BATNA: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement.
key_terms.Psychological Safety (Edmondson): Shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking. Critical for learning and innovation.
key_terms.Virtual Teams: Geographically dispersed teams using technology. Challenges: communication, trust, time zones, cultural differences.

real_world_examples: Groupthink: Bay of Pigs invasion (1961) - JFK's advisory team failed to challenge flawed plan due to group pressure
Psychological Safety: Google's Project Aristotle found psychological safety was the #1 factor in high-performing teams
Thomas-Kilmann: A team leader uses collaborating style to resolve a scheduling conflict by finding a solution that addresses everyone's needs
common_exam_topics: Identify Tuckman's stage from a team scenario
Explain how to prevent groupthink
Choose appropriate conflict management style for a situation
Describe strategies to reduce social loafing
Explain why psychological safety matters

decision_making_models.title: Decision-Making Models
decision_making_models.definition: Frameworks that describe how managers make choices among alternatives to solve problems and capitalize on opportunities.

key_terms.Rational Decision-Making Model: Ideal model: (1) Define the problem, (2) Identify decision criteria, (3) Allocate weights to criteria, (4) Develop alternatives, (5) Evaluate alternatives, (6) Select the best alternative. Assumes complete information and rationality.
key_terms.Bounded Rationality (Simon): Decision-makers have limited information, cognitive capacity, and time. They 'satisfice' (choose first acceptable option) rather than optimize.
key_terms.Intuitive Decision-Making: Making decisions based on experience, feelings, and accumulated judgment. Effective for experienced managers in time-pressured situations.
key_terms.Programmed vs Non-programmed Decisions: Programmed: routine, repetitive, handled by rules/procedures. Non-programmed: novel, unstructured, require creative solutions.
key_terms.Group Decision-Making: Advantages: diverse perspectives, more information, greater acceptance. Disadvantages: time-consuming, conformity pressure, groupthink, social loafing.

Decision-Making Biases.Confirmation Bias: Seeking information that confirms existing beliefs.
Decision-Making Biases.Anchoring Bias: Over-relying on the first piece of information encountered.
Decision-Making Biases.Availability Heuristic: Judging likelihood based on how easily examples come to mind.
Decision-Making Biases.Escalation of Commitment: Continuing to invest in a failing course of action (sunk cost fallacy).
Decision-Making Biases.Framing Effect: Decisions influenced by how options are presented.

key_terms.Decision Trees: Visual tool mapping decisions, chance events, and outcomes with probabilities and payoffs.
key_terms.Delphi Method: Structured group technique using iterative anonymous expert surveys to reach consensus.
key_terms.Cost-Benefit Analysis: Comparing total costs vs total benefits of each alternative.

key_theorists: Daniel Kahneman & Amos Tversky (Biases and Heuristics)
real_world_examples: Bounded Rationality: A hiring manager reviews 10 of 200 resumes and picks the first 'good enough' candidate rather than reviewing all applicants
Escalation of Commitment: Concorde aircraft project continued despite economic unviability because of sunk costs (the 'Concorde fallacy')
Anchoring: In salary negotiation, the first number stated becomes the anchor around which the negotiation revolves
common_exam_topics: Walk through the rational decision-making model for a scenario
Explain bounded rationality vs rational model
Identify decision-making biases in examples
Compare advantages/disadvantages of group vs individual decisions
Explain when intuitive decision-making is appropriate
